subject: Biology
text: Structural similarities and variations exist in the external morphology of larger living organisms, both plants and animals. Similarly, if one studies the internal structure, one also finds several similarities as well as differences. This chapter introduces the internal structure and functional organisation of higher plants. The study of the internal structure of plants is called anatomy. Plants have cells as the basic unit; these cells are organised into tissues and in turn, the tissues are organised into organs. Different organs in a plant show differences in their internal structure. Within angiosperms, the monocots and dicots are also seen to be anatomically different. Internal structures also show adaptations to diverse environments, reflecting the functional requirements of the plant in its specific habitat. Understanding these internal arrangements is crucial for grasping how plants conduct vital processes such as nutrient transport, structural support, and growth.

subject: Biology
section_title: The Tissue System and Epidermal Tissue
text: Tissues vary depending on their location in the plant body, and their structure and function are dependent on this location. On the basis of their structure and location, there are three types of tissue systems: the epidermal tissue system, the ground or fundamental tissue system, and the vascular or conducting tissue system. The epidermal tissue system forms the outer-most covering of the whole plant body and comprises epidermal cells, stomata, and the epidermal appendages, such as trichomes and hairs. The epidermis is the outermost layer of the primary plant body, made up of elongated, compactly arranged cells that form a continuous layer. It is usually single-layered. Epidermal cells are parenchymatous with a small amount of cytoplasm lining the cell wall and a large vacuole. The outside of the epidermis is often covered with a waxy thick layer called the cuticle which prevents the loss of water, though the cuticle is absent in roots. Stomata are structures present in the epidermis of leaves that regulate transpiration and gaseous exchange.

subject: Biology
text: Each stoma is composed of two bean-shaped cells known as guard cells which enclose the stomatal pore. In grasses, the guard cells are dumb-bell shaped. The outer walls of guard cells are thin, while the inner walls towards the stomatal pore are highly thickened. Guard cells possess chloroplasts and regulate the opening and closing of stomata. Sometimes, a few epidermal cells in the vicinity of the guard cells become specialised in their shape and size and are known as subsidiary cells. The stomatal aperture, guard cells, and surrounding subsidiary cells are together called the stomatal apparatus. The cells of the epidermis also bear various hairs. Root hairs are unicellular elongations of the epidermal cells that help absorb water and minerals from the soil. On the stem, the epidermal hairs are called trichomes. Trichomes in the shoot system are usually multicellular and may be branched, unbranched, soft, or stiff. They may even be secretory and primarily help in preventing water loss due to transpiration.

subject: Biology
text: All tissues except the epidermis and vascular bundles constitute the ground tissue. It consists of simple tissues such as parenchyma, collenchyma, and sclerenchyma. Parenchymatous cells are usually present in the cortex, pericycle, pith, and medullary rays in primary stems and roots. In leaves, the ground tissue consists of thin-walled chloroplast-containing cells and is called mesophyll. The vascular system consists of complex tissues, namely phloem and xylem, which together constitute vascular bundles. In dicotyledonous stems, cambium is present between phloem and xylem. Because of this cambium, these bundles can form secondary xylem and phloem tissues and are called open vascular bundles. In monocotyledons, the vascular bundles have no cambium and are referred to as closed because they do not form secondary tissues. When xylem and phloem are arranged in an alternate manner along different radii, the arrangement is called radial, as seen in roots. In conjoint vascular bundles, common in stems and leaves, xylem and phloem are jointly situated along the same radius.

subject: Biology
text: To understand tissue organisation, one must study transverse sections of mature organs. In a dicotyledonous root like the sunflower, the outermost layer is the epiblema, from which many cells protrude as unicellular root hairs. The cortex consists of several layers of thin-walled parenchyma cells with intercellular spaces. The innermost layer of the cortex is the endodermis, comprising a single layer of barrel-shaped cells. The tangential and radial walls of endodermal cells have depositions of water-impermeable, waxy suberin called Casparian strips. Next to the endodermis lies the pericycle, composed of thick-walled parenchymatous cells. Lateral roots and vascular cambium initiate in these cells during secondary growth. The pith is small or inconspicuous. Parenchymatous cells between the xylem and phloem are called conjunctive tissue. Usually, there are two to four xylem and phloem patches. A cambium ring later develops between them. All tissues on the innerside of the endodermis, including the pericycle, vascular bundles, and pith, constitute the stele.

subject: Biology
section_title: Monocot Root and Dicot Stem Anatomy
text: The anatomy of the monocot root is similar to the dicot root, possessing an epidermis, cortex, endodermis, pericycle, vascular bundles, and pith. However, monocot roots usually have more than six xylem bundles, a condition referred to as polyarch. The pith in monocot roots is large and well-developed, and these roots do not undergo secondary growth. In a young dicotyledonous stem, the epidermis is the outermost protective layer, covered with a thin cuticle and often bearing trichomes and stomata. The cortex between the epidermis and pericycle consists of three sub-zones. The outer hypodermis of collenchymatous cells provides mechanical strength. Below it, cortical layers consist of rounded parenchymatous cells with intercellular spaces. The innermost cortical layer is the endodermis, or starch sheath, as its cells are rich in starch grains. The pericycle is found on the inner side of the endodermis, appearing as semi-lunar patches of sclerenchyma above the phloem. Medullary rays made of parenchymatous cells sit between vascular bundles.

subject: Biology
text: In dicot stems, a large number of vascular bundles are arranged in a ring, which is a characteristic feature of this group. Each vascular bundle is conjoint, open, and possesses endarch protoxylem. Rounded parenchymatous cells with large intercellular spaces occupy the central portion of the stem, forming the pith. In contrast, the monocot stem has a sclerenchymatous hypodermis and a large number of scattered vascular bundles. Each bundle is surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheath, and the ground tissue is large and conspicuous. Vascular bundles in monocot stems are conjoint and closed. Peripheral vascular bundles are generally smaller than those located centrally. Phloem parenchyma is absent in these bundles, and water-containing cavities are typically present. This scattered arrangement contrasts sharply with the organized ring found in dicots, reflecting fundamental differences in their growth and structural support mechanisms. Monocot stems do not possess medullary rays or a clearly defined central pith due to the scattered nature of their conducting tissues.

subject: Biology
section_title: Anatomy of Dorsiventral and Isobilateral Leaves
text: The vertical section of a dorsiventral (dicotyledonous) leaf reveals three main parts: epidermis, mesophyll, and vascular system. The epidermis covers both the adaxial (upper) and abaxial (lower) surfaces with a conspicuous cuticle. The abaxial epidermis generally has more stomata. The mesophyll tissue between the epidermal layers contains chloroplasts for photosynthesis and is differentiated into palisade parenchyma and spongy parenchyma. Palisade cells are elongated and arranged vertically, while spongy cells are oval and loosely arranged with large air cavities. In an isobilateral (monocotyledonous) leaf, stomata are present on both epidermal surfaces, and the mesophyll is not differentiated into palisade and spongy layers. Grasses possess specialized adaxial epidermal cells called bulliform cells along the veins. When these cells absorb water and become turgid, the leaf surface is exposed. Under water stress, they become flaccid, causing the leaves to curl inwards to minimize water loss. Vascular bundles in leaves are seen in the veins and midrib, surrounded by thick-walled bundle sheath cells.
